message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding R-4: build_baseline_artifact lets hash_value raise raw ValueError/TypeError on non-finite or unhashable extra_inputs
File: src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:140
Description: At line 140, `input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)` is called for every extra_input. hash_value -> canonical_json uses allow_nan=False and canonical_plain refuses non-JSON-native leaves, so an extra_input containing a NaN/Inf float raises a bare ValueError ('Out of range float values are not JSON compliant') and an unhashable/uncanonicalizable leaf raises a bare TypeError. preregistration.canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:120-127) deliberately catches exactly these and re-raises a typed, contextual PreRegistrationError that names the offending input. build_baseline_artifact — the other public consumer of the same canonical hashing — does not, so the determinism harness surfaces a low-context raw exception that does not identify which extra_input was bad. Not a documented 'Raises' contract today, but the inconsistency makes a reproducibility-harness misuse hard to diagnose.
PoC: from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import build_baseline_artifact
# corpus is any valid Corpus, contents={} 
build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {}, extra_inputs={"cfg": {"thr": float('nan')}})
# -> ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant
#    (no indication that extra_input 'cfg' is the culprit)

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

100	    corpus: Corpus,
101	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
102	    *,
103	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
104	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
105	    seed: int | None = None,
106	    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
107	    trust_corpus_hash: bool = False,
108	) -> BaselineArtifact:
109	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
110	
111	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
112	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
113	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
114	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
115	    so it is reproducible.
116	
117	    ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed :func:`corpus_fingerprint` (#119). By
118	    default it is **verified** against the corpus and a mismatch raises ``ValueError`` — a
119	    stale/typo'd hash would otherwise produce a determinism record whose fingerprint and derived
120	    seed describe a *different* corpus than the one passed, silently defeating the reproducibility
121	    guarantee this harness exists to provide (#155). Pass ``trust_corpus_hash=True`` to skip the
122	    recompute (the #119 optimization) only when the caller computed the hash from this exact
123	    corpus and accepts integrity responsibility.
124	    """
125	    if corpus_hash is None:
126	        corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
127	    elif not trust_corpus_hash:
128	        # Verify the supplied hash binds the corpus actually passed (default-safe, #155).
129	        actual = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
130	        if corpus_hash != actual:
131	            raise ValueError(
132	                f"corpus_hash {corpus_hash!r} does not match the corpus "
133	                f"(corpus_fingerprint={actual!r}) — the determinism record would bind the wrong "
134	                "data; pass trust_corpus_hash=True only if you accept integrity responsibility"
135	            )
136	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
137	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
138	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
139	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
140	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
141	
142	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
143	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
144	
145	    if seed is None:
146	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
147	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
148	
149	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
150	    return BaselineArtifact(
151	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
152	    )
153

130	class LocalHmacAuthority:
131	    """A real, on-box timestamp authority binding a digest and an instant under a secret key.
132	
133	    ``stamp`` issues ``local-hmac:v1:<instant-iso>|<hmac-hex>`` where the MAC is
134	    ``HMAC-SHA256(secret, digest || instant)``. ``verify`` recomputes the MAC over the digest
135	    the engine derived from the *current* hypothesis and the instant carried in the proof, and
136	    constant-time compares. The proof therefore verifies iff the content is unchanged, the
137	    instant is unchanged, and the same secret is used — without the secret it cannot be forged
138	    for arbitrary content or time.

content.content:
32	def canonical_plain(value: Any) -> Any:
33	    """Canonicalize ``value`` into a fully JSON-native, type-faithful structure for hashing.
34	
35	    Mappings become a sorted list of ``[typed-key, value]`` pairs; bytes, sets, and any
36	    non-JSON-native leaf are tagged with their type rather than stringified. Nothing is coerced
37	    with ``str()``, so the content hash distinguishes values that share a ``str()``.
38	    """
39	    if value is None or isinstance(value, (str, bool, int, float)):
40	        return value  # JSON-native scalars; json encodes 1, "1", true, 1.0 distinctly
41	    if isinstance(value, bytes):
42	        return {"__bytes__": value.hex()}
43	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
44	        pairs = sorted(
45	            ([keytag(k), canonical_plain(v)] for k, v in value.items()), key=lambda p: p[0]
46	        )
47	        return {"__map__": pairs}
48	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
49	        return [canonical_plain(v) for v in value]
50	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
51	        return {"__set__": sorted((canonical_plain(v) for v in value), key=repr)}
52	    # A small allowlist of leaf types with a value-faithful canonical form.
53	    if isinstance(value, (_dt.date, _dt.datetime, _dt.time)):
54	        return {"__temporal__": value.isoformat()}
55	    if isinstance(value, decimal.Decimal):
56	        return {"__decimal__": str(value)}
57	    if isinstance(value, uuid.UUID):
58	        return {"__uuid__": str(value)}
59	    # Anything else is refused LOUDLY rather than repr-tagged: a default (address-based) repr
60	    # would make the digest differ every process, silently breaking the guarantee.
61	    raise TypeError(
62	        f"cannot canonicalize a leaf of type "
63	        f"{type(value).__module__}.{type(value).__qualname__!r} reproducibly — convert it to a "
64	        "JSON-native value, bytes, date/datetime/Decimal/UUID before hashing"
65	    )
66	
67	
68	def canonical_json(value: Any) -> str:
69	    """The canonical JSON text of ``value`` — stable across processes and key orderings."""
70	    return json.dumps(canonical_plain(value), sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False)
71	
72	
73	def hash_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
74	    return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
75	
76	
77	def hash_text(text: str) -> str:
78	    return hash_bytes(text.encode("utf-8"))
79	
80	
81	def hash_value(value: Any) -> str:
82	    """Stable, type-faithful content hash of an arbitrary value.
83	
84	    ``canonical_plain`` canonicalizes everything to JSON-native form first, so ``json.dumps``
85	    needs no ``default=`` coercion — anything it still cannot encode is a real bug to surface,
86	    not to silently stringify.
87	    """
88	    return hash_text(canonical_json(value))
89

    class Weird:

def test_hash_value_rejects_non_finite_float():
    # issue #B4: NaN/inf must surface, not be encoded as JSON NaN/Infinity tokens
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        hash_value(float("nan"))
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        hash_value([1.0, float("inf")])


def test_build_artifact_reserves_corpus_input_key():
    # issue #B7: an extra input named "corpus" must not clobber the corpus fingerprint
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        build_baseline_artifact(_corpus(), {}, extra_inputs={"corpus": "x"})

content.text:
The PoC reproduces exactly as the finding states: a bare `ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant: nan` with no indication that `extra_input 'cfg'` is the culprit.

I attempted to refute this finding and could not. Verified:

1. **Line 140 of determinism.py** calls `input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)` for each extra_input with no try/except wrapping.
2. **hash_value -> canonical_json** (`_canonical.py:70`) uses `allow_nan=False`, and `canonical_plain` (`_canonical.py:61`) raises a bare `TypeError` for unhashable leaves — confirming both raw-exception classes the finding names.
3. **The asymmetry is real and deliberate**: `canonical_hypothesis_digest` (`methodology/preregistration.py:120-127`) wraps the identical `hash_text(canonical_json(...))` call in `except (ValueError, TypeError)` and re-raises a typed `PreRegistrationError` that names the offending input. Its docstring (lines 105-107) even states the entrypoint "must surface a typed firewall error, not a bare ValueError/TypeError (#82)". `build_baseline_artifact` does no such wrapping.
4. **No "Raises" contract** in `build_baseline_artifact`'s docstring documents this NaN/unhashable failure mode (it only documents the `corpus_hash` mismatch and reserved-key ValueErrors).
5. **PoC executed live** and produced precisely the low-context `ValueError` with no mention of `cfg`.

Minor inaccuracy in the finding: the file path is `src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py` (not `baseline/preregistration.py`), and the cited line range is 120-127 rather than 120-127 in a baseline file — but the cited code, line numbers within that file, and behavior all match. This does not affect the substance.

The finding is a genuine, reproducible quality/consistency gap. I cannot disprove it.

input.reasoning: Refutation failed; finding confirmed by live PoC. build_baseline_artifact (src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:140) calls input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value) per extra_input with NO try/except. hash_value -> canonical_json (_canonical.py:70) uses allow_nan=False, and canonical_plain (_canonical.py:61) raises bare TypeError on non-canonicalizable leaves — both raw-exception classes the finding names. The asymmetry is real and intentional: canonical_hypothesis_digest in methodology/preregistration.py:120-127 wraps the identical hash_text(canonical_json(...)) in except (ValueError, TypeError) and re-raises a typed PreRegistrationError naming the offending input, with a docstring (105-107) stating entrypoints must surface a typed firewall error not a bare ValueError/TypeError (#82). build_baseline_artifact's docstring documents no such Raises contract for the NaN/unhashable path. Live PoC (Python 3.14, Corpus(units=(Unit('a','hello'),)), extra_inputs={'cfg': {'thr': float('nan')}}) raised exactly: ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant: nan — with zero indication that extra_input 'cfg' is the culprit. Only inaccuracy in the finding is the file path of the contrasting digest (it lives at src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py, not baseline/preregistration.py); the cited line numbers and behavior within that file are correct, so this does not undermine the finding.

content.text:
Finding R-4 survives the refutation attempt. I confirmed it with a live PoC.

The finding is accurate: `build_baseline_artifact` (src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:140) lets `hash_value` raise a bare `ValueError`/`TypeError` on non-finite or unhashable `extra_inputs`, with no context identifying the offending input. The sibling consumer `canonical_hypothesis_digest` (src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:120-127) deliberately catches exactly these and re-raises a typed, named `PreRegistrationError`, making the inconsistency real. Live PoC produced `ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant: nan` with no mention of the `cfg` input.

Only nit: the contrasting digest is at `methodology/preregistration.py`, not `baseline/preregistration.py` — but its cited line numbers and behavior match, so the finding stands.
